architecture.summary: WikiDYK is a knowledge injection benchmark and evaluation framework. The paper compares knowledge memorization across Causal Language Models (CLMs: Llama, Qwen, Gemma) and Bidirectional Language Models (BiLMs: Flan-T5, T5, RoBERTa) via continued pre-training with three training objectives (next-token prediction, synthetic QA, span prediction). A modular ensemble pipeline routes queries via a learned scope classifier to specialized BiLMs acting as external knowledge repositories, integrated with a base LLM (Llama-3.1-8B). Information flows from factual snippets → training objectives → model parameters → evaluation on multi-dimensional QA (reliability, generality, paraphrase, portability, locality).
trained_components: CLM and BiLM knowledge injection (via continued pre-training)
DeBERTa-v3-large scope classifier (binary classification for routing)

biblio.title: Bidirectional LMs are Better Knowledge Memorizers? A Benchmark for Real-world Knowledge Injection

claims.statement: Flan-T5-770M achieves match accuracy on reliability questions, compared to Llama-3.1-8B span prediction at 16.09% match accuracy, demonstrating BiLMs significantly outperform CLMs at knowledge memorization
BiLMs exhibit 23% lower accuracy in terms of reliability compared to CLMs under continued pre-training (from abstract)
Ensemble pipeline (Flan-T5-770M) improves reliability match accuracy by 6.73 percentage points over single model (52.82% vs 46.09%)
Ensemble framework improves reliability accuracy by up to 29.1% (from abstract)
WikiDYK contains 12,290 facts and 77,180 questions across five question types
Next-token prediction objective is not suitable for knowledge injection; performance is lower than static analysis baseline with catastrophic forgetting observed in locality tasks
Portability (multi-hop reasoning) shows less improvement across all models and training objectives compared to other question types

BiLM architectural advantage in bidirectional attention and span prediction objective enables richer token dependencies compared to unidirectional CLM attention with the same training objective alignment
